title: Electronic Origin of Ferromagnetic Excitations in the Candidate Spin-Triplet Superconductor CeSb2
authors: Xiaoxiao Wang, Xiaoyang Chen, Suppanut Sangphet, Yifei Fang, Yilin Wang, Chihao Li, Minyinan Lei, Nan Guo, Yuanhe Song, Rui Peng, Haichao Xu, Donglai Feng
abstract: The origin of quasi-one-dimensional (q1D) ferromagnetic (FM) excitations in the candidate spin-triplet superconductor CeSb$_2$ has remained unclear. Here we report an electronic mechanism for emergent q1D magnetism in the quasi-two-dimensional lattice of CeSb$_2$, revealed by angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES). High-resolution ARPES resolves no spin-density-wave gap on the dispersive Fermi pockets, disfavoring a nesting-driven mechanism for the q1D FM excitations. Instead, resonant ARPES reveals a pronounced selective enhancement of Ce 4$f$ spectral weight on the $C_2$-distributed Fermi pockets aligned with the Ce ladder. This observation signifies band-selective Kondo coupling that generates strongly anisotropic magnetic exchange interactions, which can naturally account for both the q1D ferromagnetic excitations and the competing magnetic orders. Our results identify a band-selective Kondo coupling mechanism for emergent low-dimensional magnetism in correlated $f$-electron systems.
summary: This work investigates the source of quasi-1D ferromagnetism in the material CeSb2 using ARPES. By showing that the magnetism is not due to Fermi surface nesting, the authors propose a band-selective Kondo coupling mechanism. This mechanism explains the strong magnetic anisotropy and provides a general model for emergent low-dimensional magnetism in correlated electron materials.
main_problem: The origin of quasi-one-dimensional (q1D) ferromagnetic (FM) excitations in the candidate spin-triplet superconductor CeSb2 remains unclear.
main_result: The study identifies band-selective Kondo coupling as the microscopic mechanism generating strongly anisotropic magnetic exchange interactions, which accounts for the emergent q1D magnetism.
method: Angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES), particularly resonant ARPES tuned across the Ce M-edge, was used to probe the electronic structure.
model_or_system: The system is CeSb2, a material crystallizing in a quasi-two-dimensional lattice and being studied as a candidate for spin-triplet superconductivity. The analysis focuses on the electronic behavior of the Ce 4f electrons interacting with the conduction bands.
key_observables: Selective enhancement of Ce 4f spectral weight on specific Fermi pockets, absence of a spin-density-wave gap, and the temperature dependence of these spectral features.
important_parameters: Low temperatures (down to 2 K), the Ce M-edge resonance condition, and the Fermi surface topology ($\alpha,  eta, \gamma, \eta$ pockets).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed spectral features are dominated by the Kondo coupling mechanism, although the possibility of sub-meV gaps is noted as a limitation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the crystal structure and Fermi surface maps, band dispersions, and photoemission intensity changes comparing off-resonance, pre-edge, and on-resonance conditions, highlighting the $4f$ spectral weight enhancement.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the mystery of the q1D magnetism in CeSb2. It then uses ARPES to rule out nesting-driven mechanisms. The core of the work presents resonant ARPES data showing band-selective $4f$ enhancement, leading to the conclusion of a band-selective Kondo coupling mechanism.
why_interesting: The identification of a band-selective Kondo coupling mechanism provides a concrete, microscopic electronic route to understanding emergent low-dimensional magnetism in correlated $f$-electron systems, which is highly relevant to strongly correlated physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. (a) Valence band dispersions near EF measured at 2 K with 27 eV photons, corresponding to the kz = π plane. (b) Corresponding band dispersions from DMFT calculations. (c) and (d) Symmetrized energy-momentum (E-k) intensity plots along ΓS1 (2 K) and ΓS2 (5 K) directions, respectively, measured with 27 eV photons. (e)- (g) Temperature-dependent symmetrized energy distribution curves (EDCs) at kβ, kγ and kα along the ΓS1 direction.

FIG. 1. (a) Crystal structure of CeSb2: (i) orthorhombic unit cell with the Ce sublattice highlighted; (ii) with the leg direction along the crystallographic b-axis (blue arrow). (b) Bulk Brillouin zone (BZ) with respect to the primitive cell. The Ce-ladder leg direction (b) corresponds to ΓS1. (c) Fermi surface map measured at 6 K with 108 eV LH-polarized photons. (d) Schematic Fermi surfaces derived from (c). (e) and (f) Band dispersions along the ΓS1 and ΓS2 direc- tions, together with DMFT calculations.

FIG. 3. Photoemission intensity along ΓS1 direction at 6 K with LH polarized photons at (a) 108 eV (oﬀresonance), (b) 115 eV (pre-edge, partial resonance), and (c) 120.7 eV (on resonance, Ce M-edge), divided by the resolution-convoluted Fermi-Dirac distribution. (d) Schematic of proposed band-selective Kondo coupling with CEF states. (e)-(f) Temperature-dependent EDCs integrated over the momentum range of panel (c). (g) Spectral intensity of 4f 1 5/2 peak over the energy window [EF−0.1 eV, EF+0.1 eV], normalized to its value at 6 K. The temperature derivative of resistivity dρ/dT is plotted to show the TK and the magnetic transitions.

FIG. 4. (a) Fermi surface map in the kx −ky plane obtained by resonance-enhanced ARPES at 20 K with hν=120.7 eV (Ce M-edge), integrated over [EF −25 meV, EF + 25 meV]. (b) MDC along the ΓS1 direction at EF; The extracted Fermi wavevectors are estimated from the Lorentzian peak positions [Table II in Ref. [35]]. (c) Schematic of the proposed band-selective Kondo coupling mechanism and re- sulting exchange anisotropy.
